title: Chemistry
intro: Chemistry questions at this level draw heavily on physical chemistry and organic chemistry, with a dense secondary layer of inorganic, analytical, and biochemical material. The most-asked answers include broad conceptual terms such as free radicals, chirality, molecular orbitals, aromaticity, and Gibbs free energy alongside instrumental techniques like mass spectrometry, NMR spectroscopy, and chromatography. Individual elements form one of the largest recurring clusters, with transition metals (copper, iron, gold, palladium, nickel) and common nonmetals (boron, sulfur, mercury, nitrogen) tested on their characteristic reactivity, oxidation states, and industrial or biological roles.

Organic chemistry contributes the single largest share of the canon: functional groups (esters, ethers, epoxides, amines), structural concepts (aromaticity, chirality, hyperconjugation, conformational analysis), and a long tail of named reactions and reagents (Diels-Alder, Wittig, Grignard, aldol condensation, Wittig, Suzuki, and dozens of others tied to specific chemists) recur across question sets. Physical chemistry supplies the thermodynamic and kinetic framework — Gibbs free energy, entropy, equilibrium constants, activation energy, and rate laws — while a closely related cluster of quantum chemistry and spectroscopy questions covers molecular orbital theory, VSEPR geometry, and the spectroscopic methods (NMR, IR, mass spec, X-ray crystallography) used to characterize compounds.

Inorganic and organometallic chemistry is anchored by coordination complexes, ligand field theory, and catalytic cycles involving palladium, platinum, and other transition metals, often overlapping with industrial processes like the Haber-Bosch and Contact processes. Analytical techniques (titration, distillation, extraction, chromatography) and materials science (polymers, graphene, fullerenes, crystal structures) each form smaller but consistent clusters, as does biochemistry, represented by amino acids, proteins, enzymes, and sugars. A long tail of eponymous chemists, named equations, and lower-frequency common-link answers — including numeric and color answerlines — rounds out the remainder of the canon.

sections.name: Organic Reactions & Synthesis
sections.blurb: Named reactions, mechanisms, and strategies for building and transforming organic molecules.

sections.name: The Elements & Simple Compounds

Diatomic gas (H2) used in fuel cells and Haber-Bosch ammonia synthesis; produced via electrolysis or steam reforming.
Radioactive actinide (U, Z=92) used as nuclear fuel, notable for isotopes U-235 and U-238.
Exothermic reaction of a fuel with oxygen producing carbon dioxide and water, releasing heat and light.
Strong oxidizing mineral acid (HNO3) made industrially via the Ostwald process; used in fertilizers and explosives.
Diatomic gas (N2) with a strong triple bond, making up most of the atmosphere and fixed via Haber-Bosch.
Reactive alkali metal (Na, Z=11) that reacts violently with water; essential electrolyte as Na+ in biology.
Group 1 elements (Li, Na, K, Rb, Cs, Fr) that are soft, highly reactive, and form +1 cations.
Hydrogen isotope with one neutron, used in NMR solvents and to study kinetic isotope effects.
Net positive charge felt by an electron after accounting for shielding by other electrons, explaining periodic trends.
Anion or compound of hydrogen bonded to a less electronegative element, acting as a strong reducing agent.
Chalcogen (Se, Z=34) below sulfur, essential in trace amounts and used in photoconductors.
Linear anion (N3-) used in click chemistry cycloadditions and as a precursor to nitrogen gas in airbags.
Atoms of the same element with different neutron counts, used in radiometric dating and kinetic isotope studies.
Heavy post-transition metal (Pb, Z=82) historically used in pipes and paint, now known for its toxicity.
Toxic metalloid (As, Z=33) below phosphorus, historically used as a poison and in semiconductor doping.
Heavy post-transition metal (Bi, Z=83) that is the heaviest stable element, used in Pepto-Bismol.
Water in which hydrogen is replaced by deuterium (D2O), used as a neutron moderator and NMR solvent.
Solid form of water with a hydrogen-bonded hexagonal lattice, less dense than liquid water.
Post-transition metal (Sn, Z=50) used in bronze alloys and as a coating to prevent corrosion.
Conjugate base of acetic acid (CH3COO-), a common buffer component and ester-forming anion.
Radioactive f-block series (Ac-Lr) including uranium and plutonium, with variable oxidation states.
Third most abundant atmospheric gas (Ar, Z=18), a noble gas used to provide inert atmospheres.
The two elements forming hydrocarbons, the structural basis of organic chemistry.
Energy change when an atom gains an electron, generally becoming more negative across a period.
Strong monoprotic mineral acid (HCl), found in gastric juice and used widely in industrial processes.
Diatomic radical (NO) that acts as a biological signaling molecule regulating vasodilation and neurotransmission.
Compounds with an oxygen-oxygen single bond, such as hydrogen peroxide, that are strong oxidizers and radical initiators.
Strong oxidizer (KMnO4) with manganese in the +7 state, used to cleave alkenes and as a disinfectant.
Strong base (NaOH) commonly called lye, used in soap making and to neutralize acids in titrations.
Pungent gas (SO2) produced by burning sulfur, an intermediate in sulfuric acid production and an air pollutant.
Transition metal (W, Z=74) with the highest melting point of any metal, used in filaments and carbides.
Linear anions (N3-) used in click chemistry cycloadditions and as a precursor to nitrogen gas in airbags.
Yellow-green diatomic halogen gas (Cl2, Z=17), used as a disinfectant and in the chlor-alkali process.
Diatomic gas (N2) with a strong triple bond, making up most of the atmosphere and fixed via Haber-Bosch.
Toxic, volatile compound (HCN) that inhibits cytochrome c oxidase and is used industrially to make acrylonitrile.
Toxic gas (H2S) with a rotten-egg smell, produced by anaerobic bacteria and found in volcanic emissions.
Energy required to remove an electron from a gaseous atom, generally increasing across a period.
Strong oxidizing anion (MnO4-) with manganese in the +7 state, used to cleave alkenes and as a disinfectant.
Transition metal (V, Z=23) used as a catalyst in sulfuric acid production and in steel alloys.
Noble gas (Xe, Z=54) that, despite its group's inertness, forms compounds like xenon hexafluoroplatinate and xenon difluoride.
Mixture of gases, mostly nitrogen and oxygen, that makes up Earth's atmosphere.
Dense, positively charged cores of atoms containing protons and neutrons, studied via nuclear and Mossbauer spectroscopy.
Measure of atomic size that increases down a group and decreases across a period due to effective nuclear charge.
Lightest alkaline earth metal (Be, Z=4), notably forming linear rather than bent covalent compounds.
Alkaline earth metal (Ca, Z=20) essential to bone structure and found in limestone as calcium carbonate.
Mineral compound (CaCO3) forming limestone and marble, and the shells of many marine organisms.
Replacement of hydrogen atoms with deuterium, used in NMR solvents and kinetic isotope effect studies.
Diatomic gas (N2) with a strong triple bond, making up most of the atmosphere and fixed via Haber-Bosch.
Energy required to remove the first electron from a neutral gaseous atom, generally increasing across a period.
Second lightest element (He, Z=2), an unreactive noble gas used in balloons and as a cryogenic coolant.
Anion (OH-) that is the conjugate base of water, defining basicity in Arrhenius acid-base theory.
Transition metal (Mo, Z=42) used in high-strength steel alloys and as a cofactor in nitrogenase enzymes.
The two most abundant gases in Earth's atmosphere, comprising roughly 99% of the air by volume.
Densest naturally occurring element (Os, Z=76), used in the oxidant osmium tetroxide for alkene dihydroxylation.
Reduction of nuclear charge felt by outer electrons due to repulsion from inner electrons, explaining periodic trends.
Tetrahedral polyatomic anion (SO4 2-) found in sulfuric acid and many mineral and biological compounds.
White pigment (TiO2) used in paints and sunscreens, and as a photocatalyst.
Helium nuclei emitted during radioactive decay, consisting of two protons and two neutrons.
Toxic transition metal (Cd, Z=48) used historically in pigments and nickel-cadmium batteries.
Stable carbon isotope with an extra neutron, used as the observed nucleus in 13C NMR spectroscopy.
Radioactive carbon isotope used to date organic materials via radiocarbon dating.
Positively charged ions formed by loss of electrons, such as metal ions in ionic compounds.
Group 16 elements (O, S, Se, Te, Po) that commonly form -2 anions and multiple oxide compounds.
Yellow oxyanion (CrO4 2-) of chromium in the +6 state, existing in equilibrium with orange dichromate.
Molecules composed of two atoms, including elemental gases like H2, N2, O2, and the halogens.
Solid form of carbon dioxide that sublimes directly to gas, used as a cooling agent.
Fundamental property of matter, positive in protons and negative in electrons, determining ionic and covalent bonding.
Lanthanide element (Eu, Z=63) valued for its strong luminescence, used in red phosphors for displays.
Visible, hot gaseous regions of combustion, whose color can identify metal ions in flame tests.
Weak but highly corrosive acid (HF) notable for etching glass and its dangerous, deep-penetrating toxicity.
Tendency of heavier main-group elements' outermost s-electrons to resist bonding, stabilizing lower oxidation states.
Anion (I-) of iodine, an essential trace nutrient and reducing agent used in starch-iodine indicator tests.
Charged species formed by loss or gain of electrons, existing as cations or anions.
Cryogenic liquid form of nitrogen gas used to achieve very low temperatures in labs.
Naturally magnetic iron oxide mineral (Fe3O4) containing both Fe(II) and Fe(III).
Uncharged subatomic particles in the nucleus that, together with protons, determine an isotope's mass number.
Compound or anion (N3-) of nitrogen bonded to a less electronegative element, as in boron nitride.
Gas (N2O) used as an anesthetic and greenhouse gas, informally called laughing gas.
General term for nitrogen oxide pollutants, including NO and NO2, contributing to smog and acid rain.
Naturally occurring mixture of hydrocarbons refined by fractional distillation into fuels and petrochemical feedstocks.
Reactive alkali metal (K, Z=19) essential as an electrolyte in biology, reacting vigorously with water.
Tendency of a substance to ignite spontaneously in air, as with white phosphorus and some organometallic reagents.
Measure of atomic or ionic size that increases down a group and decreases across a period.
Common table salt (NaCl), an ionic compound crystallizing in a cubic lattice.
Combining capacity of an atom, reflecting the number of bonds it typically forms.
Outermost electrons of an atom that participate in bonding and determine chemical reactivity.
Solid form of water with a hydrogen-bonded hexagonal lattice, less dense than liquid water.
Transition metal (Zr, Z=40) resistant to corrosion, used in nuclear reactor cladding and Ziegler-Natta-type catalysts.

sections.name: Materials, Polymers & Solid-State Chemistry
sections.blurb: Polymers, nanomaterials, crystalline solids, and other bulk and structural materials.

entries.note: Large molecules of repeating monomer units formed by addition or condensation polymerization; include plastics and rubber.
Single atomic layer of sp2-bonded carbon in a honeycomb lattice; exceptionally strong and electrically conductive.
Mixtures with particles 1-1000 nm dispersed in a continuous medium, including sols, gels, emulsions, and aerosols.
Cylindrical sp2-carbon nanostructures with exceptional tensile strength and electrical conductivity, related to graphene.
Carbon allotropes formed as closed cages, such as the soccer-ball-shaped C60 buckminsterfullerene.
Amphiphilic molecules that lower surface tension and self-assemble into micelles, used in soaps and detergents.
Microporous aluminosilicate minerals used as molecular sieves and heterogeneous catalysts, notably in cracking.
Substances with molecular order intermediate between liquid and solid, used in LCD displays.
Amorphous solid, typically silica-based, lacking long-range crystalline order and softening gradually when heated.
Spherical aggregates of surfactant molecules formed above the critical micelle concentration in aqueous solution.
Colored compounds with extended conjugation absorbing visible light, used to color fabrics and as indicators.
Colloidal networks trapping liquid within a solid matrix, exhibiting properties between solid and liquid.
Porous crystalline materials of metal nodes linked by organic ligands, used for gas storage and catalysis.
Alloy of iron and carbon, strengthened by controlling carbon content and microstructures like martensite.
Mixtures of metals, or a metal with other elements, engineered for properties like strength, corrosion resistance, or conductivity.
Covalent bonds connecting separate polymer chains, increasing rigidity as in vulcanized rubber.
Solids with atoms arranged in a repeating three-dimensional lattice, characterized by unit cells and studied via diffraction.
Aggregation of dispersed colloidal particles into larger clumps, used in water treatment to remove suspended solids.
Salts that are liquid near room temperature, used as designer solvents with negligible vapor pressure.
Colloidal suspensions of fine solid or liquid particles dispersed in a gas.
Model describing the thermodynamics of polymer solutions, accounting for the entropy and enthalpy of mixing chains with solvent.

Material made of cellulose fibers, used as a substrate in chromatography and traditionally in Bunsen's flame tests.

Elastic polymer of isoprene units, natural or synthetic, strengthened by vulcanization with sulfur crosslinks.
Energy difference between a solid's valence and conduction bands, determining whether it behaves as insulator, semiconductor, or conductor.
Crystal structure with atoms at each corner and one in the center of a cube, as in many metals.
Carbon allotrope with a tetrahedral sp3 lattice, the hardest known natural material and an electrical insulator.
Model explaining colloidal stability as a balance between van der Waals attraction and electrostatic repulsion between particles.
Process of selectively removing material from a surface using a chemical reagent, used in semiconductor fabrication.
Crystal structure with atoms at each corner and the center of each face of a cube, giving close packing.
Temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions between a rigid glassy state and a softer rubbery state.
Compounds that shuttle a reactant between two immiscible phases, enabling reactions that would otherwise proceed slowly.
Thin-film deposition technique ejecting atoms from a target material via bombardment with energetic ions.
Electric potential at a colloidal particle's slipping plane, indicating stability against aggregation.
Porous carbon material with high surface area, used to adsorb impurities in filtration and purification.
Extremely low-density porous solids formed by replacing a gel's liquid with gas, prized as insulators.
Carbon allotropes formed as closed cages, such as the soccer-ball-shaped C60 molecule.
Crystalline form of aluminum oxide, an extremely hard mineral whose gem varieties include ruby and sapphire.
Highly branched, tree-like polymers built through repeated generations from a central core.
Ability of a material, especially metals, to be drawn into wires without breaking.
Ability of a material to conduct electric current, high in metals and graphite, low in insulators like diamond.
Colloidal mixtures of two immiscible liquids, one dispersed as droplets in the other, stabilized by surfactants.
Amorphous solids, typically silica-based, lacking long-range crystalline order and softening gradually when heated.
Nanoscale gold clusters with size-dependent optical properties from surface plasmon resonance, used in sensing and imaging.
Polymer networks that absorb and retain large amounts of water while maintaining structure.
Hard, brittle steel microstructure formed by rapid cooling (quenching) that traps carbon in a distorted lattice.
Notation system describing the orientation of crystal planes using a set of three integers.
Thin-film deposition technique growing crystalline layers atom-by-atom under ultra-high vacuum for precise semiconductor fabrication.
Porous materials like zeolites with uniform pore sizes that selectively adsorb molecules by size, often used to dry solvents.
Polyamide polymers formed by condensation of diamines and diacids, used in fibers and engineering plastics.
Ratio measuring the breadth of a polymer sample's molecular weight distribution, with one indicating perfect uniformity.
Ordered molecular layers that spontaneously form on a surface through chemisorption of a functional head group.
Trade name for polytetrafluoroethylene, a highly inert, nonstick polymer used in cookware coatings.
Porous carbon material with high surface area, used to adsorb impurities in filtration and purification.
Substances that bond materials together through mechanical or chemical adhesion, often polymer-based.
Structural analog of graphite or diamond with alternating boron and nitrogen atoms, valued for hardness and thermal stability.
Soccer-ball-shaped carbon allotrope (C60) formed as a closed cage of sp2-bonded carbons.
Crystalline structures in which one molecule's lattice traps and encloses another guest molecule.
Crystal system with equal edge lengths and right-angle axes, including simple, body-centered, and face-centered forms.
Temperature at which an amorphous polymer transitions between a rigid glassy state and a softer rubbery state.
Reversible insertion of ions or molecules between layers of a host material, as in graphite-based batteries.
Industrial method converting wood into wood pulp by dissolving lignin with sodium hydroxide and sodium sulfide.
Geometric factor used to calculate the electrostatic lattice energy of an ionic crystal.
Mixtures of metals engineered for properties like strength, corrosion resistance, or conductivity, such as steel or bronze.
Polyamide polymer formed by condensation of diamines and diacids, used in fibers and engineering plastics.
Crystal structure type (ABX3) important in solar cell materials and superconductors.
Generation of electric charge in certain crystals in response to applied mechanical stress.
Water-soluble polymer of repeating ethylene oxide units, used in drug delivery and as a phase-transfer catalyst.
Silicon dioxide (SiO2), the main component of sand and glass and the common chromatography stationary phase.

Relative stereochemical orientation of substituents along a polymer chain, described as isotactic, syndiotactic, or atactic.

sections.name: Biochemistry & Biomolecules
sections.blurb: Amino acids, sugars, proteins, and nucleic acids at the interface of chemistry and biology.

entries.note: Building blocks of proteins bearing amino and carboxyl groups; the 20 standard types link via peptide bonds.
Carbohydrates like glucose and sucrose, classified by carbon count and existing in ring or open-chain forms.
Pharmaceutical compounds studied for structure-activity relationships, synthesis routes, and mechanisms of biological action.
Protein catalysts that lower activation energy for biological reactions, often described by Michaelis-Menten kinetics.
Nitrogenous waste product (CO(NH2)2) excreted by mammals, historically the first organic compound synthesized from inorganic precursors.
Cyclic amino acid whose secondary amine restricts backbone flexibility, disrupting alpha helices in proteins.

Polymers of amino acids linked by peptide bonds, folding into functional secondary and tertiary structures.
Polysaccharide of beta-linked glucose units forming plant cell walls, the most abundant organic polymer on Earth.
Method building peptides on an insoluble resin, adding one protected amino acid at a time before final cleavage.
Lipids with a four-fused-ring carbon skeleton, including cholesterol and hormones like testosterone and estrogen.

Reagent that reacts with free amino groups to produce a purple color, used to detect and visualize amino acids.
Macrocyclic ligands with four pyrrole rings that bind metal ions, forming the core of heme and chlorophyll.
Alkaloid stimulant found in coffee and tea that blocks adenosine receptors.
Steroid lipid with a four-fused-ring skeleton, a component of cell membranes and precursor to steroid hormones.
Simple six-carbon sugar central to cellular respiration, existing in equilibrium between open-chain and cyclic pyranose forms.
pH at which a molecule like an amino acid or protein carries no net electric charge.
Sugars and polysaccharides built from carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, serving as energy storage and structural material.

Sulfur-containing amino acid whose thiol side chain forms disulfide bonds that stabilize protein structure.
Covalent linkages between two cysteine thiol groups that stabilize protein tertiary and quaternary structure.

Alkaloid natural product historically used to treat malaria, isolated from cinchona bark.
Natural products built from isoprene units, found in essential oils and as precursors to steroids.
Common protein secondary structure coiled into a helix stabilized by backbone hydrogen bonds.
Rearrangement of an intermediate in the Maillard reaction that converts a glycosylamine into a ketosamine.
Noncovalent or covalent association between molecules, such as a substrate binding an enzyme's active site.
Anion of citric acid, a key intermediate in the citric acid cycle and a common buffer and chelating agent.
Double-helical nucleic acid encoding genetic information via base pairing between complementary strands.
Long carboxylic acid chains, saturated or unsaturated, that combine with glycerol to form triglycerides and phospholipids.
Highly branched polysaccharide of glucose that serves as the primary energy storage form in animals.
Iron-containing porphyrin cofactor that binds oxygen in hemoglobin and myoglobin.
Amino acid with an imidazole side chain that acts as a versatile acid-base catalyst in enzyme active sites.
Proteins containing iron-sulfur clusters that shuttle electrons in metabolic pathways like the electron transport chain.
Five-carbon diene building block of natural rubber and terpenes.
Complex aromatic polymer providing structural rigidity in plant cell walls, removed from wood pulp by the kraft process.
Reaction between amino acids and reducing sugars under heat that produces browning and flavor compounds in cooked food.
Application of chemistry and physics principles to cooking, including techniques like spherification and emulsification.
Change in optical rotation as a sugar's cyclic anomers interconvert through the open-chain form in solution.
Polymers of nucleotides, namely DNA and RNA, that store and transmit genetic information.
Tetrahedral polyatomic anion (PO4 3-) forming the backbone of DNA and RNA and the core of ATP.
Aromatic amino acid with a phenolic side chain that can be phosphorylated in cell signaling.

sections.name: Analytical Techniques & Separations
sections.blurb: Chromatography, titration, distillation, and other methods for isolating and quantifying substances.

entries.note: Separation technique exploiting boiling-point differences, ranging from simple to fractional and vacuum distillation.
Separation exploiting differential affinity for stationary versus mobile phases; includes gas, liquid, and thin-layer forms.
Technique finding unknown concentration via controlled addition of a reagent of known concentration to an equivalence point.
Separation technique partitioning a solute between two immiscible liquid phases by relative solubility.
Formation of an insoluble solid from a solution, often used to isolate or identify ions.
Separation technique volatilizing a sample and carrying it through a column via an inert carrier gas.
Purification technique forming solid crystals from a saturated solution as it cools or solvent evaporates.
Separation technique using a solvent that travels up a plate coated with adsorbent, visualized via retention factor.
Separation technique spinning a sample at high speed to sediment components by density.
Technique measuring heat flow associated with phase transitions like melting or the glass transition as a sample is heated.
Technique measuring fluctuations in scattered laser light to determine particle size in solution.
Imaging technique using electron beams instead of light to resolve structures at near-atomic resolution.
Purification technique dissolving a solid in hot solvent and allowing pure crystals to form as it cools.
Separation technique sorting molecules by size as they pass through a porous gel, larger molecules eluting first.
Design of chemical processes that reduce or eliminate hazardous substances, emphasizing atom economy and safer solvents.
Imaging technique scanning a sharp tip across a surface to map topography at atomic resolution.
Technique separating charged biomolecules like DNA or proteins by size as they migrate through a gel under an electric field.
Time a compound takes to travel through a chromatography column, used to identify substances by comparison to standards.
Separation technique exploiting a specific binding interaction, such as antibody-antigen, to isolate a target molecule.
Process of washing a compound off a chromatography stationary phase using a mobile phase solvent.
Separation technique partitioning a solute between two immiscible phases by relative solubility.
Ventilated enclosure that draws away hazardous vapors, protecting laboratory workers during chemical procedures.

Distillation setup that removes water as it forms, driving condensation reactions like ester or imine formation to completion.
Separation technique using a semipermeable membrane to remove small molecules from a solution of larger ones.
Separation technique applying a field perpendicular to flow to sort particles by size or mass without a stationary phase.
Technique identifying metal ions by the characteristic color they impart to a flame when heated.
Technique measuring a sample's mass change as it is heated, revealing decomposition, dehydration, or oxidation events.
Sudden, violent boiling of a superheated liquid lacking nucleation sites, prevented in distillation with boiling chips.
Laboratory gas burner producing an adjustable flame, used for heating and flame tests, named for Robert Bunsen.
Instruments spinning samples at high speed to sediment components by density.
Separation technique passing a mixture through a vertical column of stationary phase to purify by differential retention.
Technique determining the elemental composition of a compound by combustion and measurement of resulting gases.
Separation technique passing a mixture through a porous medium to separate solids from liquids or gases.
Separation technique pumping a liquid sample under high pressure through a column packed with fine stationary phase.
Technique measuring heat released or absorbed during a binding interaction to determine thermodynamic parameters.
Laboratory instruments used to measure and transfer precise volumes of liquid.
Technique boiling a reaction mixture while condensing and returning vapor to the flask, sustaining reaction temperature without loss of solvent.
Degree to which a compound is held by a chromatography stationary phase relative to the mobile phase.
Laboratory device using reduced pressure and rotation to efficiently remove solvent from a mixture.
Dual-manifold vacuum and inert gas apparatus used to handle air- or moisture-sensitive compounds.
Stepwise dilution of a solution by a constant factor, used to prepare a range of known concentrations.
Separation technique using reduced pressure to speed the passage of liquid through a filter, isolating a solid.
Titration-based method for determining dissolved oxygen concentration in a water sample.

sections.name: Chemists & the History of Chemistry
sections.blurb: Scientists, eponymous processes, and discoveries that shaped the field's development.

entries.note: American physicist-chemist who formalized chemical thermodynamics, defining Gibbs free energy and the phase rule.
American organic chemist who developed retrosynthetic analysis and won the Nobel Prize for total synthesis methodology.
American chemist who developed the electronegativity scale and valence bond theory, winning two unshared Nobel Prizes.
German chemist who studied catalysis and reaction rates, developed the Ostwald process, and won the 1909 Nobel Prize.
German chemist known for the Hofmann elimination and rearrangement, and for advancing coal-tar dye chemistry.
American chemist who developed the Langmuir adsorption isotherm and studied surface chemistry and monolayers.
American organic chemist renowned for total syntheses of complex natural products and co-developer of the Woodward-Hoffmann rules.
American chemist who introduced Lewis structures and the electron-pair theory of acids and bases and covalent bonding.
American chemist who developed asymmetric epoxidation and dihydroxylation reactions and pioneered click chemistry.
English scientist who established the laws of electrolysis and coined terms like electrode, anode, and cathode.
German chemist known for the Claisen condensation and Claisen rearrangement of allyl vinyl ethers.
German chemist who developed Schlenk line techniques for handling air-sensitive compounds under inert atmosphere.
French chemist who established the law of conservation of mass and helped overturn phlogiston theory.
German chemist who elucidated sugar and purine structures and developed the Fischer projection and Fischer esterification.
Dutch physicist who derived an equation of state correcting the ideal gas law for real-gas volume and attraction.
German physicist who contributed the probabilistic interpretation of the wavefunction and co-developed the Born-Oppenheimer approximation.
Swedish chemist who proposed the original acid-base theory and the equation relating rate constant to temperature.
German chemist who formulated the Nernst equation and the third law of thermodynamics.
British chemist who used X-ray crystallography to determine the structures of penicillin, vitamin B12, and insulin.
German chemist Hermann Emil Fischer, who elucidated sugar and purine structures and developed the Fischer esterification.
British chemist who developed Wilkinson's catalyst and elucidated the structure of ferrocene, winning the 1973 Nobel Prize.
German chemist who developed the Wittig reaction converting carbonyls and phosphorus ylides into alkenes.
American chemist who introduced Lewis structures and the electron-pair theory of acids and bases and covalent bonding.
International body that standardizes chemical nomenclature, atomic weights, and terminology.
Dutch chemist who pioneered stereochemistry with the tetrahedral carbon model and won the first Nobel Prize in Chemistry.
English chemist credited with isolating oxygen gas and discovering carbonated water.
Principle established by Lavoisier stating that mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
American theoretical chemist who co-developed the Woodward-Hoffmann rules governing pericyclic reaction stereochemistry.
German chemist who co-invented spectroscopy with Kirchhoff and developed the burner that bears his name.
Japanese chemist who developed asymmetric hydrogenation catalysts, sharing the 2001 Nobel Prize with Knowles and Sharpless.
Japanese chemist known for the Mukaiyama aldol reaction using silyl enol ethers with Lewis acid catalysis.
